mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the STATUS_REG in the provided code example?

options.text: To check if the device is ready for data transfer
To store the character to be printed
To interrupt the CPU when ready

mcqs.explanation: The STATUS_REG contains status information about the device, specifically the ready/busy bit that the CPU polls to determine when the device is ready to accept data.

mcqs.question: What I/O technique is demonstrated in the code example?
mcqs.explanation: The code uses polling (busy-waiting) where the CPU repeatedly checks the status register until the device becomes ready, consuming CPU cycles during the wait.

mcqs.question: What memory addressing technique is used in the code with #define statements?
mcqs.explanation: The #define statements create memory addresses for device registers (STATUS_REG and DATA_REG), which is characteristic of memory-mapped I/O where devices appear as memory locations.

mcqs.question: What happens when the condition '(STATUS_REG & 0x1) == 0' becomes false?

options.text: The device is ready to accept data
The device needs to be reset

mcqs.explanation: The expression checks the least significant bit of the status register. When (STATUS_REG & 0x1) != 0, it means the device is ready to accept data, so the loop exits and data can be written to DATA_REG.

mcqs.question: What is the main disadvantage of the I/O method shown in the code?
mcqs.explanation: Polling (busy-waiting) wastes CPU cycles because the processor is constantly checking the device status instead of performing other useful work during the wait period.
